en.extract:
The white-fronted honeyeater (Purnella albifrons) is a medium-sized bird species endemic to Australia. Mainly distributed throughout arid and semi-arid landscapes. The white-fronted honeyeater has distinct colourings with a white face, black or brown upper chest with white speckles and yellow panels on their brown wings.
The white-fronted honeyeater was described by the English bird artist John Gould in 1841 and given the binomial name Glyciphila albifrons. The specific epithet combines albus meaning 'white' with frons meaning 'forehead' or 'front'. The white-fronted honeyeater was formerly in the genus Phylidonyris, but is now classified as their own genus, Purnella. The generic name was chosen to honour the oologist and collector, Herbert A. Purnell.


Ecology


Diet
White-fronted honeyeaters (as the name suggests) primarily consume nectar from flowers. Studies have been undertaken to expand this field of knowledge and it has been identified that when nectar is not available or in minimal supply, white-fronted honeyeaters may consume a range of other materials such as seeds, plant matter and insects. This level of variation within the diet allows white-fronted honeyeaters to be able to adapt to different environments and survive in arid areas. The diet of white-fronted honeyeaters has been determined by previous observational studies as well as the analysis of gizzard contents.


Behaviour
The white-fronted honeyeater moves quickly throughout landscapes; foraging for food and avoiding predators. The species regularly frequents tall shrub species, such as Eremophila shrubs and flowering mallee plants.
White-fronted honeyeaters breed in small colonies. This process ensures that white-fronted honeyeaters can warn their neighbours of the same species of potential predators when they are at their most vulnerable. The female white-fronted honeyeater constructs their nest from materials that are easy to find within their landscape, and that can add support and structure to the nest. These materials include a range of organic matter such as grasses, spider webs, roots, bark and plant stems. They are woven together to create a cup-like nest which is then lined with an extra layer to provide warmth and comfort to the chicks. This nest lining is composed of a mix of wool, cotton threads, fur and plant material. Nests are generally constructed in the lower branches of shrubs.
The female white-fronted honeyeaters nurtures and broods the eggs, providing warmth and safety. When the eggs hatch, both the female and male white-fronted honeyeaters work tirelessly to provide sufficient food for their young.


Predatory threats
White-fronted honeyeaters must remain vigilant of predators at all times including when brooding their young. Other bird species can be a major threat to nesting white-fronted honeyeaters as species such as butcherbirds, currawongs and corvids are known for raiding the nests of other bird species. These nest raiders are known to consume the eggs or young chicks of honeyeaters within Australia. Some snake species, such as the northern brown snake, are also known as nest predators, as they also consume the young chicks and eggs of the white-fronted honeyeater.
Accidents can happen during breeding season resulting in the destruction of the eggs or the nest. Heavy rain events can destroy nests or dislodge the nest from the shrub where it is located. Honeyeater species in general have been found to occasionally destroy eggs.


Conservation
White-fronted honeyeaters are currently classified as 'least concern' under the IUCN red list. The species is secure in the majority of States and Territories in Australia, including SA, VIC, NSW, QLD, NT and WA.
White-fronted honeyeaters are not found in Tasmania due to the geographical distance from mainland Australia and the landscape composition of Tasmania.


Gallery

}}The '''white-fronted honeyeater''' ('''''Purnella albifrons''''') is a medium-sized bird species [[Endemism|endemic]] to [[Australia]]. Mainly distributed throughout [[arid]] and [[Semi-arid climate|semi-arid]] landscapes. The white-fronted honeyeater has distinct colourings with a white face, black or brown upper chest with white speckles and yellow panels on their brown wings.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|last=BirdLife Australia|title=White-fronted Honeyeater {{!}} BirdLife Australia|url=https://birdlife.org.au/bird-profile/white-fronted-honeyeater#:~:text=The%20endemic%20White-fronted%20Honeyeater,western%20arid%20zone%20of%20Queensland.|access-date=2021-06-07|website=birdlife.org.au|language=en}}</ref>

The white-fronted honeyeater was described by the English bird artist [[John Gould]] in 1841 and given the [[binomial name]] ''Glyciphila albifrons''.<ref>{{ cite journal | last=Gould | first=John | author-link=John Gould | year=1840 | title=''Glyciphila albifrons'' | journal=Proceedings of the Zoological Society of London | volume=Part 8 | pages=160–161 | url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/30571599 }} Although bearing the year 1840 on the title page, the volume did not appear until 1841.</ref> The [[specific epithet]] combines ''albus'' meaning 'white' with ''frons'' meaning 'forehead' or 'front'.<ref>{{cite book | last=Jobling | first=James A. | year=2010| title=The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names | url=https://archive.org/details/helmdictionarysc00jobl | url-access=limited | publisher=Christopher Helm | location=London | isbn=978-1-4081-2501-4 | page=[https://archive.org/details/helmdictionarysc00jobl/page/n38 38] }}</ref> The white-fronted honeyeater was formerly in the genus ''[[Phylidonyris]]'',<ref name="peters">{{cite book|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14482546|title=Check-list of Birds of the World|publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology|year=1986|editor-last=Paynter|editor-first=Raymond A. Jr|volume=12|place=Cambridge, Massachusetts|page=429}}</ref> but is now classified as their own [[genus]], '''''Purnella'''''.<ref name=mathews>{{ cite journal | last=Mathews | first=Gregory M. | author-link=Gregory Mathews | year=1914 | title=New species | journal=Austral Avian Record | volume=2 | issue=5 | pages=110–116 [111] | url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/34804717 }}</ref> The generic name was chosen to honour the [[Oology|oologist]] and collector, Herbert A. Purnell.<ref name=mathews/><ref>{{cite book | last=Jobling | first=James A. | year=2010| title=The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names | url=https://archive.org/details/helmdictionarysc00jobl | url-access=limited | publisher=Christopher Helm | location=London | isbn=978-1-4081-2501-4 | page=[https://archive.org/details/helmdictionarysc00jobl/page/n324 324] }}</ref>

== Ecology ==

=== Diet ===
White-fronted honeyeaters (as the name suggests) primarily consume [[nectar]] from flowers.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|last=Watson|first=David M.|date=17 July 1995|title=The Importance of Mistletoe to the White-fronted Honeyeater ''Phylidonyris albifrons'' in Western Victoria|url=https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.1071/MU97021?journalCode=temu20|journal=Emu – Austral Ornithology|volume=97|issue=2|pages=174–177|doi=10.1071/MU97021|via=Taylor & Francis Online|url-access=subscription}}</ref> Studies have been undertaken to expand this field of knowledge and it has been identified that when nectar is not available or in minimal supply, white-fronted honeyeaters may consume a range of other materials such as seeds, plant matter and insects.<ref name=":1" /> This level of variation within the diet allows white-fronted honeyeaters to be able to adapt to different environments and survive in arid areas. The diet of white-fronted honeyeaters has been determined by previous observational studies as well as the analysis of [[gizzard]] contents.<ref name=":1" />

=== Behaviour ===
The white-fronted honeyeater moves quickly throughout landscapes; foraging for food and avoiding predators. The species regularly frequents tall shrub species, such as ''[[Eremophila (plant)|Eremophila]]'' shrubs and flowering [[Mallee (habit)|mallee]] plants.<ref>{{Cite journal|author1=Recher, H. F. |author2= Davis, W. E. Jr.|date=January 2002|title=Foraging profile of a Salmon Gum woodland avifauna in Western Australia|url=https://researchrepository.murdoch.edu.au/id/eprint/27353/1/Salmon_Gum_woodland_avifauna.pdf|journal=Journal of the Royal Society of Western Australia|volume=85|page=106|via=Research Repository}}</ref>

White-fronted honeyeaters breed in small colonies.<ref name=":0" /> This process ensures that white-fronted honeyeaters can warn their neighbours of the same species of potential [[Predation|predators]] when they are at their most vulnerable. The female white-fronted honeyeater constructs their nest from materials that are easy to find within their landscape, and that can add support and structure to the nest. These materials include a range of [[organic matter]] such as grasses, spider webs, roots, bark and plant stems. They are woven together to create a cup-like nest which is then lined with an extra layer to provide warmth and comfort to the chicks.<ref name=":0" /> This nest lining is composed of a mix of wool, cotton threads, fur and plant material. Nests are generally constructed in the lower branches of shrubs.<ref name=":0" />

The female white-fronted honeyeaters nurtures and broods the eggs, providing warmth and safety. When the eggs hatch, both the female and male white-fronted honeyeaters work tirelessly to provide sufficient food for their young.<ref name=":0" />

== Predatory threats ==
White-fronted honeyeaters must remain vigilant of predators at all times including when brooding their young. Other bird species can be a major threat to nesting white-fronted honeyeaters as species such as [[butcherbird]]s, [[currawong]]s and [[Corvidae|corvids]] are known for raiding the nests of other bird species.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web|last=Australian honeyeaters|date=June 9, 2021|title=Australian Honeyeaters (Meliphagidae)|url=https://www.encyclopedia.com/environment/encyclopedias-almanacs-transcripts-and-maps/australian-honeyeaters-meliphagidae}}</ref> These nest raiders are known to consume the eggs or young chicks of honeyeaters within Australia. Some snake species, such as the [[Pseudonaja nuchalis|northern brown snake]], are also known as nest predators, as they also consume the young chicks and eggs of the white-fronted honeyeater.<ref name=":0" />

Accidents can happen during breeding season resulting in the destruction of the eggs or the nest. Heavy rain events can destroy nests or dislodge the nest from the shrub where it is located.<ref name=":2" /> Honeyeater species in general have been found to occasionally destroy eggs.<ref name=":2" />

== Conservation ==
White-fronted honeyeaters are currently classified as 'least concern' under the [[IUCN Red List|IUCN red list]].<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021"/> The species is secure in the majority of States and Territories in Australia, including [[South Australia|SA]], [[Victoria (Australia)|VIC]], [[New South Wales|NSW]], [[Queensland|QLD]], [[Northern Territory|NT]] and [[Western Australia|WA]].<ref name=":0" />

White-fronted honeyeaters are not found in [[Tasmania]] due to the geographical distance from mainland Australia and the landscape composition of Tasmania.
